_meta.note: CURATED SUBSET — a hand-built, high-signal slice of real Meta / Taboola / Google / TikTok ad policy + FTC guidance, not the full corpora. Deliberately conservative: it flags the patterns that actually get affiliate ads rejected. Replacing this with a live policy-RAG over the full policy documents is the top what's-next item (see README).

severity.flag: allowed but risky — likely to draw review or a soft rejection; fix before scaling spend
severity.block: will almost certainly be rejected or get the account actioned; must fix before launch

_meta.disclaimerAlignment: FTC results/typicality rules reference the same disclosure wording as the advertorial (FTC_BASELINE / FTC_RESULTS in agents/advertorial.ts).

shared.reason: FTC: unqualified guarantees imply a certain outcome and need clear terms + substantiation.
shared.fix: Qualify it (e.g. '30-day money-back guarantee') and don't imply a guaranteed result.

shared.reason: FTC: a quantified results claim must reflect typical results or carry an atypical-results disclosure.
shared.fix: Add 'Results not typical; individual results vary.' and avoid implying everyone loses that amount.

shared.reason: FTC: specific earnings claims require typicality substantiation.
shared.fix: Remove the specific figure or disclose that results are not typical and require effort.

shared.reason: FTC: 'risk-free' implies no cost or obligation; often misleading with auto-ship / trial terms.
shared.fix: State the real terms — shipping cost, trial length, and how to cancel.

shared.reason: FTC: fabricated scarcity/countdowns are deceptive if not real.
shared.fix: Only state scarcity that is genuinely true and enforced.

shared.reason: FTC/FDA: proof and endorsement claims require you to actually hold that substantiation.
shared.fix: Only claim it if you hold the evidence; otherwise soften to 'formulated with studied ingredients.'

shared.reason: Unrealistic speed-of-result claims are a top rejection trigger across platforms.
shared.fix: Drop the timeframe or reframe as 'many users start to notice…' without a hard promise.

meta.reason: Meta prohibits claims that a product cures, treats, or reverses a medical condition.
meta.fix: Reframe as general wellness/support and drop the medical-condition claim entirely.

meta.reason: Meta prohibits ads that imply or attempt to generate negative self-perception about the viewer's body.
meta.fix: Use third-person, general framing ('a common concern') instead of targeting the viewer's body.

meta.reason: Unrealistic body-transformation phrasing draws Meta's personal-attributes / body-image scrutiny.
meta.fix: Describe the mechanism honestly (e.g. 'supports metabolism') rather than 'melts fat.'

meta.pattern: \bdoctors?\s+(?:hate|don'?t want you to know|are stunned|are shocked|are baffled)\b
meta.reason: Sensational 'doctors hate this / are stunned' framing is deceptive and disallowed.
meta.fix: Remove the fake-authority hook; lead with a real, specific benefit instead.

meta.reason: 'Miracle/magic' claims signal exaggerated efficacy and are commonly rejected.
meta.fix: Replace with a concrete, supportable benefit.

meta.reason: 'Without diet or exercise' implies effortless results — an unrealistic-expectations violation.
meta.fix: Drop the effortless promise; note it works alongside normal habits.

meta.reason: Unsubstantiated superlatives ('#1 in America') need proof.
meta.fix: Remove the superlative or cite the specific ranking source.

taboola.reason: Taboola discourages classic 'one weird trick' clickbait as low-quality/misleading.
taboola.fix: Lead with the actual insight instead of a curiosity-gap clickbait phrase.

taboola.reason: Over-sensational shock language is a native-policy quality flag.
taboola.fix: Keep the curiosity but ground it in a specific, truthful detail.

taboola.reason: Before/after transformation framing is heavily restricted for health/beauty natives.
taboola.fix: Avoid before/after; describe the approach and set realistic expectations.

taboola.reason: Advertorials that masquerade as breaking news / a study without basis are deceptive.
taboola.fix: Do not imply journalism or a study you can't cite; keep the 'Sponsored' framing honest.

google.reason: Google's healthcare/misleading policy prohibits treatment/cure claims for these conditions.
google.fix: Remove the condition-specific claim; general wellness language only.

google.reason: Google flags absolute effectiveness/approval guarantees as misleading.
google.fix: Remove the absolute; describe the offer without promising a certain outcome.

tiktok.reason: TikTok prohibits weight-loss / fasting / body-image ads (esp. to general audiences).
tiktok.fix: This offer likely can't run body-weight angles on TikTok; pivot to a non-body benefit or drop TikTok.

tiktok.reason: TikTok bans content promoting negative body image or idealized body outcomes.
tiktok.fix: Remove body-shape targeting; focus on how the product feels/works, not the viewer's body.
